metadata.notes: Wingspan standard overhaul: removed legacy sections format, added rules_citations with key rulings, validated content against official rulebook.

subtopics.title: Components
subtopics.content:
#### Cards
Let's go through the cards first. The big deck contains 183 dragon cards — 148 are full-grown dragons ready to use, and 35 are hatchlings that require sustained investment (caching resources and tucking cards) before they reach their full potential.

There are also 75 cave cards — these are played to excavate spaces in your caverns before dragons can move in. Think of them as preparing a home for your dragons.

Finally, you'll find 10 double-sided objective tiles (giving 20 possible objectives). You'll use 4 per game.

#### Player Components
Each player gets a player mat showing 3 cavern rows with 4 slots each. The three rows are the Crimson Cavern (red, top), the Golden Grotto (yellow, middle), and the Amethyst Abyss (purple, bottom). Column 1 in each cavern comes pre-excavated — the other 3 columns need cave cards before dragons can live there.

You also get an adventurer meeple (this moves through your caverns when you explore), a guild token (tracks your position on the Dragon Guild board), and 8 small player markers for claiming guild tile benefits.

#### Resources & Tokens
The resource tokens come in 4 types: Meat (red), Gold (yellow), Crystals (purple), and Milk (white) — 100 tokens total across all types.

There are 55 egg miniatures — each one is worth 1 VP at game end and also used as escalation costs when you explore the same cavern multiple times.

You'll find 45 coin tokens. Important: coins are NOT resources — they represent your actions. Each coin lets you take 1 action on your turn.

There are also 20 multiplier tokens for scoring.

#### Shared Components
The Dragon Guild board sits near the main play area — it has a circular track plus 4 double-sided guild tiles (use the 2-3 player side or the 4-5 player side depending on your count). There's a card display board for the face-up dragon market, a round tracker, and 17 Automa cards for solo play.

subtopics.title: Setup
subtopics.content:
#### Setting Up the Game
Each player takes a player mat — look at it and notice that column 1 of each cavern is already excavated (marked with a cave symbol). The other 3 columns in each row are empty and will need cave cards before dragons can be placed there.

Take your adventurer meeple, guild token, and 8 player markers and set them nearby.

Shuffle the dragon card deck and set up the face-up display on the card display board. Shuffle the cave card deck separately.

Choose the correct side of the Dragon Guild tiles based on your player count — 2-3 player side or 4-5 player side. Place 4 random objective tiles face-up where everyone can see them.

Now deal each player 5 dragon cards and 3 cave cards, plus 2 objective tiles. Look at your dragon cards and keep 3, discarding 2 face-down. Look at your 2 objective tiles and keep 1, discarding the other.

Choose 3 starting resources — any combination of meat, gold, crystals, and milk. You could take 3 of one type, or 1 each of three types, or any mix you want.

Each player starts with 0 eggs and 6 coins for Round 1.

Randomly determine the first player.

At this point, you should have your player mat with column 1 pre-excavated in all 3 caverns, 3 dragon cards and 3 cave cards in hand, 1 secret objective, 3 resources of your choice, 0 eggs, and 6 coins ready to spend.

subtopics.title: Actions & Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
#### How a Round Works
Wyrmspan lasts exactly 4 rounds. At the start of each round, you receive 6 coins plus 1 egg. Then players take turns spending coins on actions until everyone has spent all their coins (or chosen to stop).

#### Your Three Actions
Every action costs 1 coin. On your turn, you spend 1 coin and choose one of these three:

**1. Excavate** — Play a cave card from your hand into the leftmost empty slot of any cavern row. This prepares the space for a dragon. The cost depends on which column you're excavating: Column 2 costs just 1 coin. Column 3 costs 1 coin plus 1 egg. Column 4 costs 1 coin plus 2 eggs. When the cave card lands, its "when played" bonus activates immediately.

Remember: you MUST excavate a space before placing a dragon there. Column 1 is pre-excavated, but columns 2-4 need cave cards first.

**2. Entice Dragon** — Play a dragon card from your hand into an excavated-but-empty slot. Pay the dragon's resource cost (shown on the card), then place it in the leftmost excavated empty slot of the matching cavern. If the dragon has a "when played" ability, resolve it now.

**3. Explore Cavern** — Your adventurer moves left-to-right through one cavern, activating each dragon's ability in sequence. At the end of the row, you gain the cavern's printed explore reward. Exploring the same cavern multiple times per round costs extra: the first exploration is free (just 1 coin), the second costs 1 coin + 1 egg, and the third costs 1 coin + 2 eggs.

#### Coins Are Your Actions
You get 6 coins minimum each round. Some abilities and guild bonuses can give you extra coins — and those extra coins are extremely valuable because each one is an additional action. Unspent coins at the end of a round are WASTED. Spend them all.

#### Hand Limit
At the end of your turn, you can hold a maximum of 9 total items — that's dragon cards plus cave cards plus resource tokens combined. If you're over 9, discard down to 9.

subtopics.title: The Three Caverns
subtopics.content:
#### Crimson Cavern (Red — Top Row)
This cavern favors meat-eating dragons. When you explore the Crimson Cavern, your adventurer activates each dragon left to right, then you gain the mat's explore reward: resources plus advancement on the Dragon Guild track.

This is your primary resource engine. Getting dragons here early means more resources every time you explore — and those resources fuel everything else you want to do.

#### Golden Grotto (Yellow — Middle Row)
This cavern favors gold-loving dragons. The explore reward here gives you dragon cards from the deck or display, plus Dragon Guild advancement.

The Golden Grotto is your card flow and tucking engine. Tucked cards under your dragons are worth 1 VP each at game end. Dragons here that tuck cards on exploration generate VP every single time you explore.

#### Amethyst Abyss (Purple — Bottom Row)
This cavern favors crystal-consuming dragons. The explore reward gives you cave cards plus Dragon Guild advancement.

The Amethyst Abyss fuels your excavation pipeline — explore to gain cave cards, then use those cave cards to excavate more spaces. It's also the best home for hatchling dragons, since hatchlings need sustained feeding and the Abyss's self-sustaining card flow supports that.

#### How Exploration Works
When you explore, your adventurer meeple enters the leftmost space of the chosen cavern and moves right, space by space. Each dragon you pass through activates its ability in sequence. After the last dragon (or the last space), you gain the cavern's printed explore reward.

A full cavern with 4 dragons means 4 ability activations plus the end-of-row reward — that's massive value for a single action. This is why focusing on filling one cavern deep is so powerful.

subtopics.title: Dragon Guild & Abilities
subtopics.content:
#### The Dragon Guild Track
The Dragon Guild board has a circular shared track. You advance on it through exploration (printed icons on the cavern rewards), card abilities, and other effects.

When your guild token moves, you gain the bonus of every space you land on AND pass through — so multiple advances in a row can cascade into a chain of bonuses.

The brown spaces on the track are special: when you land on one, place one of your 8 player markers on the Dragon Guild tile to claim a benefit. You only get 8 markers total, so later placements let you claim the more valuable guild tile slots.

Guild tile slot priority: the 4th slot (end-game VP scoring) is the most valuable — claim it first if you can. The 5th slot is next best, then the 3rd (extra action or coin). The 1st and 2nd slots offer discounted actions.

#### Dragon Ability Types
Dragons have different ability timings, and understanding these is key to building your engine:

**When Played** — Triggers once, immediately when you place the dragon. A one-time bonus.

**When Activated** — Triggers every single time you explore that cavern. These are the engine-building core of the game — a dragon that gives you a resource on every exploration gets more valuable the more you explore.

**Once Per Round** — Triggers consistently once each round, regardless of how often you explore.

**End of Game** — Awards VP at final scoring based on conditions.

#### Hatchlings
35 of the 183 dragons are hatchlings. These require you to cache resources or tuck cards onto them over multiple turns. When you reach the hatchling's threshold (usually 3 items cached or tucked), its powerful third-line ability fires once.

Hatchlings are high investment, high payoff. Place them early (Round 1-2) if you're going to commit — placing a hatchling in Round 4 won't give you enough time to feed it.

#### Dragon Personalities
Dragons have personalities: Aggressive, Helpful, Playful, and Shy. Some dragons score bonus VP for being adjacent to matching personalities in the same cavern.

#### Key Rules to Remember
Coins are NOT resources — abilities that discount or convert resources don't apply to coins.

You can trade any 2 resource tokens for 1 resource of any type at any point during your turn. This 2-for-1 conversion works as many times as you need.

At end-game, every 4 leftover items (cards in hand plus unspent resources) convert to 1 VP. Remaining items that don't form a complete set of 4 are worth nothing.

subtopics.title: Scoring & End Game
subtopics.content:
#### When the Game Ends
The game ends after all players complete Round 4.

#### Adding Up Your Score
Your final VP comes from these sources:

**Dragon VP** — The point value printed on each dragon you've placed in your caverns.

**Eggs** — 1 VP each for every egg remaining on your mat.

**Tucked cards** — 1 VP each for every card tucked under your dragons.

**Cached resources** — 1 VP each for resources cached on hatchling dragons.

**Round objectives** — The 4 objective tiles score progressively: later rounds are worth more VP than earlier ones.

**Dragon Guild tile markers** — VP from the guild tile slots you claimed, especially the end-game scoring slots.

**End-game dragon abilities** — Some dragons award VP at final scoring based on conditions.

**Leftover conversion** — Every 4 leftover items (cards + resources) convert to 1 VP.

#### Tiebreaker
If scores are tied, the player with the most remaining resources wins.

subtopics.title: Beginner Strategy
subtopics.content:
#### Key Principles

**Coins equal actions — spend them all.** You get 6 coins minimum each round, and that means 6 actions. Unspent coins at round end are completely wasted. Never leave coins on the table.

**Follow the excavate-then-entice-then-explore rhythm.** First, play cave cards to excavate spaces. Then, place dragons in those excavated spaces. Finally, explore the cavern to activate all your dragons and collect the row reward. Empty caverns explored means wasted actions.

**Focus one cavern deep.** A cavern with 4 dragons gives you 4 ability activations plus the explore reward — that's massive value per action. Two dragons in each of 3 different caverns gives you far less. Pick your primary cavern and fill it to 4 dragons as fast as you can.

**Start with the Crimson Cavern.** Resource-generating dragons in the Crimson Cavern fuel everything else. If your opening hand has a cheap meat-cost dragon, the Crimson Cavern is almost always your best first home.

**The Dragon Guild matters more than you think.** It's not optional flavor — it's a core resource and scoring engine. Advancing on the guild track gives you bonuses, resources, and access to end-game VP slots on the guild tile. Prioritize it.

**Eggs are precious.** Each egg is worth 1 VP at game end, and you also need them as escalation costs for exploring and excavating deeper columns. Don't spend eggs carelessly — every egg used for costs is 1 VP you won't score.

#### Common Mistakes
- Exploring a cavern before placing any dragons in it — you get the row reward but miss all the activations
- Leaving unspent coins at round end — those are wasted actions you'll never get back
- Ignoring the Dragon Guild track — it's a major source of resources and end-game VP
- Spreading dragons evenly across all 3 caverns instead of going deep in one
- Not reading all 4 objective tiles during setup — you need to plan around them from turn 1

subtopics.title: First-Game Walkthrough
subtopics.content:
#### If You've Played Wingspan
Wyrmspan shares about 85% of its DNA with Wingspan, reimplemented with some key refinements:

**No dice.** Resources from exploration are predictable — no birdfeeder luck.

**Coins equal actions.** You get 6 per round (not the shrinking 8/7/6/5 of Wingspan). You can also earn bonus coins for extra actions.

**Two-step placement.** You need to excavate a space (play a cave card) before you can place a dragon there. That's two separate actions instead of Wingspan's one.

**Dragon Guild.** A circular shared track with cascading bonuses. There's nothing like this in Wingspan.

**Hatchlings.** 35 dragons that require multi-turn investment before their big payoff.

**Left-to-right exploration.** Wingspan activates birds right-to-left; Wyrmspan goes left-to-right.

#### Your First Game Setup
You've got 3 cavern rows on your mat, 4 slots each, with column 1 pre-excavated in every row. You've kept 3 of your 5 dealt dragons, kept 1 of 2 objectives, and chosen 3 starting resources. You have 6 coins and 0 eggs. Let's play.

**Round 1 (6 Coins = 6 Actions)**

*Action 1*: Excavate the Crimson Cavern's column 2 — play a cave card from your hand into that slot and gain its bonus. That costs 1 coin.

*Action 2*: Entice a dragon into Crimson column 1 (the pre-excavated slot). Pay its resource cost and place it. If it has a "when played" ability, resolve it now. 1 coin.

*Action 3*: Entice another dragon into Crimson column 2 (the one you just excavated). Pay its cost and place it. 1 coin.

*Action 4*: Explore the Crimson Cavern! Your adventurer moves left to right — your 2 dragons both activate their abilities, then you collect the cavern's explore reward (resources + guild advancement). 1 coin.

*Actions 5-6*: Use your remaining 2 coins to excavate or entice in another cavern, or explore the Crimson again (second exploration costs 1 coin + 1 egg — and you got an egg from the round start). Spend all your coins!

**Rounds 2-3: Building the Engine**
Fill your focus cavern to 4 dragons — that makes every exploration extremely valuable. Advance on the Dragon Guild track whenever possible. Keep an eye on the 4 objective tiles and steer toward the ones worth the most points.

**Round 4: Scoring Push**
Play high-VP dragons if you have them. Lay eggs for end-game points. Complete any hatchlings you've been feeding. Check your objective one last time.

**First Game Target**: Aim for 70-85 VP. Focus on filling one cavern deep rather than spreading thin across all three.

subtopics.title: Advanced Strategy
subtopics.content:
#### Wingspan vs Wyrmspan: Strategic Differences
If you're coming from Wingspan, here's what changes strategically:

No dice means precise resource planning — you always know exactly what you'll get from exploration.

Bonus coins equal extra actions, and this is the single most important strategic lever in the game. A player with 8 actions per round crushes a player with only 6.

Two-step placement (excavate then entice) means slower engine development. Your engine needs to be online by Round 2 or you'll fall behind.

The guild track is a major VP and resource source — there's no Wingspan equivalent, and ignoring it is a serious mistake.

Hatchlings are mini-quests requiring sustained investment over multiple rounds.

#### Engine Types

**Crimson Focus (Safest)**: Fill the Crimson Cavern with resource-generating dragons. Every exploration cascades into more resources, which fuel your next moves. This is the most reliable approach.

**Golden Focus (Card Engine)**: Build a tucking engine in the Golden Grotto — every tucked card is 1 VP, and golden dragons often generate card flow for combos. Great card-draw synergy.

**Amethyst Focus (Hatchlings)**: The Amethyst Abyss creates a self-sustaining cave card pipeline. Pair this with hatchling dragons for the highest ceiling strategy, though it has the highest variance too.

**Recommended approach**: Go deep in 1 cavern — aim for 4 dragons by Round 3. Add a light second cavern (1-2 dragons) for support.

#### Guild Tile Priority
When you land on a brown space and claim a guild tile slot, here's the priority:

1. **4th slot (end-game scoring)** — Claim this first. It's the highest-value slot.
2. **5th slot (end-game scoring)** — Second priority.
3. **3rd slot (extra action/coin)** — Tempo boost that pays off immediately.
4. **1st/2nd slots (discounted actions)** — Smallest benefit.

At 4-5 players, race for the brown spaces — the guild tile gets contested hard and early claims matter.

#### Coin Optimization
Spend all 6+ coins every round — unspent coins are wasted turns. The column 4 special ability (spend 3 items to gain 1 coin) is very powerful because that extra coin is an extra action.

Seek dragons and guild bonuses that grant coins. A player consistently getting 7-8 actions per round has a massive advantage over someone stuck at 6.

#### Hatchling Strategy
Place hatchlings early — Round 1 or 2 — in the Amethyst Abyss where you can feed them through exploration. Most hatchlings need 3 cached items to trigger their powerful one-time ability.

If you're going to commit to a hatchling, commit early. Drafting one in Round 3-4 rarely gives you enough time to reach the threshold.

#### Round Objectives
Study all 4 objectives during setup — they shape your entire game plan. Later round objectives are worth more VP than earlier ones, so it can be worth sacrificing Round 1-2 performance to dominate Rounds 3-4.

Scoring zero on any objective is an automatic 0 VP for that round. Avoid zeros — even 1 point is better than nothing.

#### Player Count Adjustments
- **2-3 Players**: The Dragon Guild is less contested, so you have more flexibility on timing. The dragon card display refreshes slowly — plan around what's available rather than hoping for new draws.
- **4-5 Players**: Guild competition is fierce. The display churns faster. Watch what your opponents are drafting and adjust.

#### Key Decisions
1. **Setup**: Choose 3 starting resources that support both your opening dragons and your first guild advance.
2. **Round 1**: Establish the excavate-entice-excavate-entice-explore rhythm in your focus cavern.
3. **Round 2**: Commit to your focus cavern — you want 4 dragons in it by end of this round or early Round 3.
4. **Round 3**: Race to claim the 4th guild tile slot for end-game scoring.
5. **Round 4**: Stop building, start scoring. Every action should directly generate VP.

key_rulings.text: Coins represent actions, not resources. Abilities that gain, convert, or discount resources do not apply to coins. Coins cannot be traded using the 2-for-1 resource conversion.
You may only place a dragon in a space that has been excavated (has a cave card). Column 1 of each cavern is pre-excavated at game start; columns 2-4 require cave cards.
The first time you explore a cavern each round is free (1 coin only). The second exploration of the same cavern costs 1 coin + 1 egg. The third costs 1 coin + 2 eggs.
At the end of each round, any unspent coins are lost. They do not carry over to the next round. Spend all your coins each round to maximize your actions.
At the end of your turn, you may not have more than 9 total items in your personal supply (dragon cards + cave cards + resource tokens combined). Discard excess items of your choice.
At any point during your turn, you may exchange any 2 resource tokens for 1 resource token of any type. This conversion can be done multiple times per turn.

key_rulings.topic: Dragon placement — leftmost excavated empty slot
key_rulings.text: When enticing a dragon, you must place it in the leftmost excavated-but-empty slot of the matching cavern. You cannot skip spaces.

Hatchling dragons require you to cache resources or tuck cards onto them. When the number of cached/tucked items reaches the hatchling's threshold (shown on the card), the hatchling's powerful third-line ability triggers once.
When you excavate the 4th column of any cavern, you unlock a special ability: spend 3 items (any combination of resources, dragon cards, or cave cards) to gain 1 coin. This can be used once per turn.
At the end of the game, every 4 leftover items (cards in hand plus unspent resources) convert to 1 victory point. Remaining items that do not form a complete set of 4 are worth nothing.

key_rulings.topic: 'In this cave' means one row only
key_rulings.text: 'In this cave' refers to the specific row in which the dragon is located (Crimson Cavern, Golden Grotto, or Amethyst Abyss), NOT your entire player mat. Do not apply these abilities across all three rows.
Cached resources are not in your supply, and you are not able to spend them. Cached resources remain on the dragon card until end of game and are worth 1 VP each unless a card specifies otherwise.

key_rulings.topic: Any resource icon means ONE resource
key_rulings.text: When you see the any-resource icon, you gain any ONE resource of your choice (meat, milk, crystal, or gold). You do NOT gain one of each resource. Coins are not resources and cannot be selected.

key_rulings.topic: Free excavation waives egg costs too
key_rulings.text: When a card ability says you may 'excavate a cave for free,' this negates BOTH the coin cost of the Excavate action AND the egg cost associated with placing a cave card in columns 3 or 4.
